steps.title: Welcome to the Lab
steps.instruction:
Welcome to Enterprise Grade Marmalade. In this lab you will explore and extend a Central Code Prompt Repository (CCPR).

The lab has pre-cloned the ccpr_root template into your workspace. This is a real CCPR template that provides the folder structure, file conventions, and starter content for building your organization's AI playbook.

Your challenge: explore the template, understand its structure, then customize it with your own organization's standards, prompts, skills, and agents. Follow the guide panel as you advance through the slides.

steps.title: Explore the CCPR Template
steps.instruction:
The ccpr_root template has been pre-cloned into your workspace. Open the Explorer sidebar and expand the ccpr_root folder. Browse its directory structure: instructions, prompts, skills, and agents directories.

This is the starting point for every organization's CCPR. Notice how it already has starter files and a clear organizational structure.

steps.title: Identify Your Gaps
steps.instruction:
Now compare the CCPR template to your actual needs. In Copilot Chat, ask:

Look at the ccpr_root folder in my workspace. Then consider: what coding conventions, architectural decisions, and team-specific knowledge would a new developer need to learn about a typical project? List at least five things that are not yet captured in this CCPR template.

Keep this list handy. You will encode these gaps into the CCPR throughout the lab.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Explorer: Ctrl+Shift+E
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Map File Types to Your Needs
steps.instruction:
The slides introduced four file types: .prompt.md, .instructions.md, SKILL.md, and .agent.md. Open the CCPR template and find examples of each type. Note how each is structured.

Now map your gap list items to these file types. Open a new file and draft a quick mapping. For example:

• "Always use parameterized SQL queries" = instruction
• "How to deploy to staging" = skill
• "Security review checklist" = agent
• "Generate API endpoint boilerplate" = prompt template

This mapping becomes your build plan for the rest of the lab.
steps.tip: If an item could fit more than one type, choose the type that delivers the most value with the least maintenance.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the editor tab for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N

steps.title: Review the CCPR Structure
steps.instruction:
Open the Explorer and review the ccpr_root folder structure. The slide shows the recommended layout, which the template already implements.

Consider: does the template structure work for your organization, or would you need to customize it? Common customizations include adding subdirectories for team-specific content or removing categories your org does not use.

If you want to customize, use Copilot Chat in agent mode:

Look at the ccpr_root folder structure. Add a new subdirectory under instructions/ for our organization's API design patterns. Include a README explaining the directory's purpose.

steps.title: Verify Discoverability
steps.instruction:
Review the folder tree. Ask yourself:

• Can a new developer find what they need in under 30 seconds?
• Are the folder names descriptive enough without READMEs?
• Is there a clear place for every file type?

If anything feels hard to find, reorganize now. Discoverability is the top priority for a shared repo.

steps.title: Inspect from the Terminal
steps.instruction:
Open the Terminal and run a directory listing to see the ccpr_root structure from the command line:

PowerShell:
Get-ChildItem -Recurse ccpr_root -Name

Bash:
find ccpr_root -type f | head -30

This view is helpful for verifying folder depth, spotting misplaced files, and confirming your structure matches the expected layout.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• Explorer: Ctrl+Shift+E
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Apply Naming Conventions
steps.instruction:
Review the files in the ccpr_root template. Check them against the naming rules from the slide:

• Are all file names in kebab-case?
• Does every file include the correct type suffix (.prompt.md, .instructions.md, .agent.md)?
• Does each file address a single concern?

The template should already follow these conventions. If you added new files in the previous step, make sure they follow the same pattern.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Explorer: Ctrl+Shift+E

steps.title: Plan Your Distribution Model
steps.instruction:
The slides present four distribution approaches: git submodules, npm packages, symlinks, and copy-on-clone. Your challenge: choose the best approach for a team of your size and stack.

Open Copilot Chat and explore the trade-offs:

Compare git submodules vs npm packages for distributing shared prompt files across 10 repositories. My team uses Node.js and TypeScript. Which approach would you recommend and why?

There is no single right answer. The best choice depends on your team's existing workflows.

steps.title: Write a Distribution Script
steps.instruction:
Whichever model you chose, write the setup command or script. For example, if you chose git submodules, create the git submodule add command. If you chose copy-on-clone, draft a sync script.

Add the setup instructions to your repo's README.md so consuming teams know exactly how to integrate.

Hint: the slide shows example commands for each approach. Adapt one to your repo structure.
steps.tip: Many organizations start with copy-on-clone for simplicity and migrate to submodules or npm packages as the prompt library matures.

steps.title: Test Your Setup Command
steps.instruction:
Open the Terminal and test the distribution approach you chose. If you selected git submodules:

cd ccpr_root && git status

If you drafted a sync script, run it and verify it copies files to the expected locations. Validate the result by listing the directory contents to confirm everything landed correctly.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Review or Create CODEOWNERS
steps.instruction:
Check whether the ccpr_root template already includes a .github/CODEOWNERS file. If it does, review it and customize the team handles for your organization. If not, create one that assigns review responsibility for each directory.

Think about who should own each area:

• Language instructions: language standards team
• Framework instructions: framework leads
• Agents: platform engineering
• Skills: architecture team

Use GitHub team handles or placeholder names.

steps.title: Create a PR Template
steps.instruction:
Create a .github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md that enforces the review process from the slide. Your template should ask contributors to provide:

• A description of what changed
• The intended audience for this prompt or instruction
• Before/after evidence showing how Copilot output changed

Challenge: can you write a template that makes it easy for contributors to include test evidence?

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the editor tab for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N

steps.title: Explore Existing Prompt Templates
steps.instruction:
Browse the prompts/ directory in the ccpr_root template. Open the existing prompt files and examine their structure: YAML frontmatter, input variables, and requirement sections.

Now create your own .prompt.md file. Choose the most common task your team performs (code review, test generation, or documentation are good starting points).

Your prompt template should include:
• YAML frontmatter with description and mode
• A clear structure with numbered expectations
• At least one ${input:variable} placeholder
• Rules that enforce your team's standards

Save it in the prompts/ directory with a descriptive kebab-case name.
steps.tip: The slide's code review template is a strong reference. Study its structure, then adapt it for your team's specific review criteria.

steps.title: Test Your Prompt Template
steps.instruction:
Open Copilot Chat and type / to open the prompt picker. Find your new template and run it with sample input.

Evaluate the output:
• Does it follow the structure you defined?
• Does it enforce your team's standards?
• Would a junior developer get value from this template?

Iterate on the template until you are satisfied with the output quality.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Write a Language Instruction File
steps.instruction:
Create a scoped instruction file for your team's primary language. Save it in instructions/languages/ with the correct naming pattern.

Your instruction file must include:
• An applyTo glob pattern in the YAML frontmatter
• At least five rules that encode your team's coding standards
• Rules that are specific to your organization, not generic best practices

Challenge: can you write rules that would catch the most common code review feedback your team gives?

steps.title: Add a Framework Instruction File
steps.instruction:
Create a second instruction file scoped to your team's primary framework. Save it in instructions/frameworks/.

Remember from the slide: scoped instructions are additive. A developer working on a React TypeScript file would get both the TypeScript language instructions and the React framework instructions applied simultaneously.

Make sure your framework instructions do not duplicate rules already in the language file. Each file should address a single concern.

steps.title: Configure File Associations
steps.instruction:
Open Settings and search for files.associations. Add associations for *.prompt.md and *.instructions.md so VS Code treats them as Markdown files with proper syntax highlighting.

This ensures your team gets a consistent editing experience across all CCPR file types.

steps.title: Verify Layered Instructions
steps.instruction:
Open Copilot Chat and ask:

What instructions are active for this workspace? List them and summarize their rules.

Confirm that both your language and framework instruction files are loaded. Then test them: ask Copilot to generate a small piece of code and verify the output follows both sets of rules.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N
• Settings: Ctrl+,
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Encode Institutional Knowledge
steps.instruction:
Create a SKILL.md file that captures one piece of tribal knowledge your team relies on. The slide uses a deployment pipeline as an example, but choose something relevant to your organization:

• Your CI/CD pipeline stages and requirements
• Your database migration process
• Your API versioning strategy
• Your incident response procedure

Save it in the skills/ directory. Include enough detail that a new developer could understand the process without asking a senior engineer.

steps.title: Test the Skill
steps.instruction:
Ask Copilot Chat a question that your skill should be able to answer. For example, if you wrote a deployment pipeline skill:

How do I deploy a new service to production? What approvals are needed?

Does Copilot reference your skill file and provide organization-specific guidance? Or does it give generic advice? If it misses your skill, check that the file is in the correct location and has a clear description at the top.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Create a Custom Agent
steps.instruction:
Design an .agent.md file for a role your team frequently needs. The slide shows a security reviewer; consider what role would deliver the most value for your team:

• A security reviewer that checks for OWASP vulnerabilities
• A DevOps assistant that helps with CI/CD configuration
• An accessibility reviewer that audits UI components
• A documentation writer that enforces consistency

Your agent definition should include a description, tool access, behavioral instructions, and references to relevant skills.

steps.title: Invoke the Agent
steps.instruction:
Open Copilot Chat and switch to your new agent using the @ mention or the mode selector. Give it a task that exercises its specialty.

Evaluate the response:
• Does it stay in character for its defined role?
• Does it reference the skills and instructions you linked?
• Would this agent save your team time compared to a generic Copilot prompt?

Refine the agent definition until its output consistently meets your expectations.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Add CI Validation
steps.instruction:
Your central prompt repo needs automated quality checks. Create a GitHub Actions workflow at .github/workflows/validate-prompts.yml that validates your prompt and instruction files.

The slide shows a starting point. Your challenge: extend it to check for:
• Valid YAML frontmatter in all .prompt.md and .instructions.md files
• Kebab-case naming conventions
• Required fields like description in prompt files

Use Copilot to help you write the validation scripts, but make sure the checks match your team's conventions.
steps.tip: Start with simple checks you can trust, then add more sophisticated validation over time. A CI pipeline that catches real issues is worth more than one that checks everything but nobody trusts.

steps.title: Validate Locally First
steps.instruction:
Open the Terminal and run a quick local validation before committing your workflow. Check that your CCPR files follow naming conventions:

PowerShell:
Get-ChildItem -Recurse ccpr_root -Include *.prompt.md,*.instructions.md,*.agent.md | Select-Object Name

Bash:
find ccpr_root -name '*.prompt.md' -o -name '*.instructions.md' -o -name '*.agent.md'

Verify all file names use kebab-case and have the correct suffixes.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Final Challenge: The Getting Started Checklist
steps.instruction:
The slide presents an eight-item getting started checklist. Review your work against it:

• CCPR forked from the ccpr_root template
• copilot-instructions.md customized with org-wide baseline rules
• 2-3 instruction files for languages and frameworks
• At least one prompt template
• At least one SKILL.md file
• CODEOWNERS and PR template
• Distribution model documented in README
• Baseline metrics defined for measuring impact

How many items can you check off? Fill in any gaps now.

steps.title: Define Your Metrics
steps.instruction:
The one item teams most often skip: baseline metrics. Open your README.md and add a Measuring Impact section.

Define at least three metrics you will track before and after rolling out your central prompt repo. The slide suggests: acceptance rate, code review cycle time, onboarding time-to-first-PR, style violation rate, and developer satisfaction.

Choose the metrics that matter most to your team and write down how you will measure them.

steps.title: Search Prompt Commands
steps.instruction:
Open the Command Palette and type prompt. Browse the available prompt-related commands — you should see options for running prompt files, managing chat participants, and working with Copilot's prompt features.

These commands are what your team members will use daily to interact with the CCPR content you built.

steps.title: Review Your Changes
steps.instruction:
Open Source Control to see all the files you created and modified during this lab. Review the staged and unstaged changes — this is the collection of CCPR content you built today.

In a real workflow, you would create a branch, commit these changes, and open a pull request using the PR template you created earlier.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• Command Palette: Ctrl+Shift+P
• Source Control: Ctrl+Shift+G

steps.title: Lab Complete!
steps.instruction:
Congratulations! You have completed Enterprise Grade Marmalade.

You explored and customized a CCPR (Central Code Prompt Repository), including:

• Exploring the ccpr_root template structure
• Customizing scoped instruction files for languages and frameworks
• Creating reusable prompt templates for common tasks
• Adding a skill file encoding institutional knowledge
• Building a custom agent with a specialized role
• Configuring governance with CODEOWNERS and PR templates
• Adding CI validation for prompt file quality
• Planning distribution and measurement strategies

Next steps: MCP Maximizer to extend Copilot's tool capabilities, or Multi-Agent Multi-Repo Roundup for applying your CCPR across repositories.
